text:
You be seated. I didn't get to see the man that did that singing. That was a real good job though. Thank you. My favorite song. I guess you know the author, Mr. Clibborn, Booth-Clibborn? [A man says, "I know of him."] Yes, sir. It was wonderful. How many enjoyed that song? Oh, that was just… That's next to: Only Believe. That was a good one.
And I was talking to some friends of mine just as I come up, Mr. and Mrs. Doble. I was asking about the little baby. Ten years ago when I was here, the Lord gave me a vision, seeing her holding a boy baby in her arms. And she taken sick.
And years passed, but the boy baby is here now. And so, she was back there. I met them just behind the stage. And my little boy Joseph walks around to meet her just now. And they were having a little time of fellowship.
text_no:
Sett dere ned. Jeg fikk ikke se mannen som sang, men det var en virkelig god opptreden. Takk skal du ha. Det er min favorittsang. Jeg antar du kjenner forfatteren, Mr. Clibborn, Booth-Clibborn? [En mann sier, "Jeg kjenner til ham."] Ja, sir. Det var fantastisk. Hvor mange likte den sangen? Å, det var bare… Den er nesten på høyde med "Only Believe." Det var en bra en.
Jeg snakket med noen venner av meg akkurat da jeg kom opp, Mr. og Mrs. Doble. Jeg spurte om den lille babyen. For ti år siden, da jeg var her, ga Herren meg en visjon hvor jeg så henne holde en guttebaby i armene. Og hun ble syk.
Årene gikk, men nå er guttebabyen her. Jeg møtte dem bak scenen, og min lille gutt Joseph gikk bort for å møte henne akkurat nå. De hadde en liten stund med fellesskap.

text:
And Brother Bosworth, the old gentleman, you know him, he used to… He's got a great sense of humor. He says, "Brother Branham, you know what fellowship is?"
I said, "Well, I think so."
Said, "It's two fellows in one ship, makes the fellowship." And that's about right. Baptist closed communion.
And so there has been some complaints about the speaking not vibrating or something or other back, but they said tonight it had cleared up. Is the sound all right, better back there tonight? Can you hear better back in the back? Can, raise up your hands, can hear all right. That's fine. Fine. That's good.
text_no:
Og Bror Bosworth, den gamle herren, du kjenner ham, han pleide å... Han har en stor sans for humor. Han sier, "Bror Branham, vet du hva fellesskap er?"
Jeg sa, "Vel, jeg tror det."
Han sa, "Det er to kamerater i én båt, det skaper fellesskap." Og det er ganske riktig. Baptistisk lukket nattverd.
Det har vært noen klager på at lyden ikke vibrerer eller noe slikt, men de sa at det hadde bedret seg i kveld. Er lyden bedre der bak i kveld? Kan du høre bedre der bak? Kan du, løft hendene hvis du kan høre godt. Det er fint. Flott. Det er bra.

text:
Tomorrow afternoon, there's a… Tonight, pardon me, tonight, we have made a promise… That's the reason I could come in without coming under the anointing and so forth, and talk with you a little while. We have given Friday night now…
The best of our calculation of the ministers here counting, has been about between seven and eight hundred accepted Jesus Christ as Savior since we've been in the city. And so to that, we're very happy.
What we been trying to do is to have faith to find favor with God. And then if we can put first things first, then we think God will give us favor now for the next two or three nights for great healing services. We believe that He will do it. Course we're still asking sinners to come to Christ. And so the…
text_no:
I morgen ettermiddag, nei, i kveld, har vi gitt et løfte... Det er grunnen til at jeg kunne komme inn uten å være under salvelsen og snakke med dere en stund. Vi har nå satt av fredag kveld...
Etter vår beste beregning av prestene her, har rundt sju til åtte hundre personer tatt imot Jesus Kristus som Frelser siden vi kom til byen. Dette gjør oss svært glade.
Det vi har forsøkt å gjøre, er å ha tro for å finne Guds velvilje. Og hvis vi kan prioritere riktig, tror vi at Gud vil gi oss velvilje de neste to til tre nettene for store helbredelsesmøter. Vi tror at Han vil gjøre det. Selvfølgelig ber vi fortsatt syndere om å komme til Kristus.

text:
Mr. Mercier here, one of my associates, the tape recording, him and Mr. Goad, tells me that the books are out, but they will take the orders if you want them. And they'll be sent to you. And we didn't bring but just a few of them, and we had to distribute them between Tacoma and here. So they will take the orders on through, but they…
I don't think they have any books. Is that right, Leo? Don't have no books, but you still have tapes and a few of the pictures left, is that right? Just a few of the pictures left.
Now, I do not, (as I said in the beginning of the meetings), I do not… I'm not a book salesmen. I buy those books from Mr. Lindsay. I get forty cents less. Well, time you bring them across the country and the losses and then we don't … in Canada we never put a price on anything. We just set it out there. I know what it's supposed … what it cost us. But if the people wants it, can't afford it by paying for it, take it anyhow. We give it to you. So therefore we…
text_no:
Mr. Mercier her, en av mine medarbeidere, sammen med Mr. Goad, informerer meg om at bøkene er utsolgt, men de vil ta imot bestillinger hvis du ønsker dem. De vil bli sendt til deg. Vi tok med bare noen få bøker, og vi måtte fordele dem mellom Tacoma og her. Så de vil ta imot bestillinger, men de...
Leo, stemmer det at dere ikke har flere bøker? Men dere har fortsatt noen lydbånd og få bilder igjen, ikke sant? Bare noen få bilder igjen.
Som jeg sa i begynnelsen av møtene, er jeg ikke en bokselger. Jeg kjøper disse bøkene fra Mr. Lindsay med en rabatt på førti cent. Men når vi transporterer dem over hele landet og tar med i beregningen tapene, så setter vi aldri pris på noe i Canada. Vi legger dem bare ut. Jeg vet hva det koster oss. Men hvis folk ønsker det og ikke har råd til å betale, kan de ta det likevel. Vi gir det til dem. Derfor...

text:
And these pictures, that belongs to the Douglas Studios in Houston, Texas, and it's copyrighted (a member of the American Photographers Association). And one hangs in the religious hall in Washington, DC. And under it is written, "The only supernatural Being that was ever scientifically proven to be taken." The only supernatural Being that was ever scientifically proven.
And so they're just something, little tokens of the meeting. I don't know what they … what they even pay for them when they get them. But ever what it is, that's what you get it for. And so, they're not us, it goes right straight back to the studios, you see.
text_no:
Disse bildene tilhører Douglas Studios i Houston, Texas, og er opphavsrettslig beskyttet (medlem av American Photographers Association). Et av bildene henger i en religiøs hall i Washington, DC. Under det står det skrevet: "Det eneste overnaturlige Vesen som noensinne er vitenskapelig bevist." Det eneste overnaturlige Vesen som noensinne er vitenskapelig bevist.
Disse bildene er små minner fra møtene. Jeg vet ikke hva de koster, men det du betaler for dem, går direkte tilbake til studioene.

text:
So we don't… They're copyrighted. And just bring them along, and we don't sell books or do things. We're not here… We don't want your… We say, "Write to us." We don't mean to get your address so we can ask you to support something. We don't have nothing to support. The only thing you can support me by is your prayers. That's all.
I don't have any radio program, no television programs; just have a little office there with… We mail out many handkerchiefs and things across the world daily. And my expenses runs me about seventy-five to a hundred dollars a day. That's a year round at home. And so just enough till I have to stay on the field all the time.
text_no:
Vi har ikke noen salgsvarer, og selger verken bøker eller annet. Vi er heller ikke her for å be dere om økonomisk støtte eller deres adresser for å sende forespørsler. Deres bønner er den eneste støtten vi søker.
Jeg har ingen radioprogrammer eller TV-programmer; bare et lite kontor hvor vi daglig sender ut mange lommetørklær og andre ting over hele verden. Mine utgifter ligger på omtrent 75 til 100 dollar per dag, året rundt. Det er akkurat nok til at jeg må være på reisefot hele tiden.

text:
Somebody here sometime ago offered me a great big offering of several thousand dollars. I refused to take it. I said, "I don't need that. Just put it in foreign missions." And the Lord will supply for me just as the days go by. That's the way I want to live: just for the day. I don't know about tomorrow. He takes care of that for me when tomorrow comes. But I just have today to live for.
Now, tonight we're going to pray for every person that has a prayer card, tonight. And then tomorrow afternoon, Mr. Rasmussen, a former pastor of this city, a man that I have learned to love with real Christian love and respects… And I know all that knows Brother Rasmussen says the same. And he's speaking here each afternoon.
And then tomorrow night… Billy says the people are not here so that they can give prayer cards to them enough when he gets here. We don't want to interrupt with the other services. So tomorrow afternoon prayer cards will be given out after two o'clock, tomorrow afternoon, here in the auditorium. And perhaps Sunday, the closing, also.
text_no:
For en tid tilbake tilbød noen meg en stor pengegave på flere tusen dollar. Jeg takket nei og sa: "Jeg trenger ikke det. Gi det heller til utenlandsmisjonene." Herren vil sørge for meg når dagene går. Det er slik jeg ønsker å leve: bare for i dag. Jeg kjenner ikke morgendagen, men Han tar seg av den når den kommer. Jeg har bare i dag å leve for.
I kveld skal vi be for hver person med et bønnekort. I morgen ettermiddag vil Mr. Rasmussen, en tidligere pastor i denne byen og en mann jeg har lært å elske med ekte kristen kjærlighet og respekt, tale. Alle som kjenner Bror Rasmussen, sier det samme som meg. Han taler her hver ettermiddag.
I morgen kveld... Billy sier at folk ikke er her for å få utdelt bønnekort når han kommer. Vi vil ikke forstyrre de andre møtene, så i morgen ettermiddag vil bønnekort bli delt ut etter klokken to her i auditoriet. Muligens vil det også være slik på avslutningen på søndag.

text:
Now, I hope if there's someone here that doesn't understand English, why that someone sitting next to them will tell them. Tomorrow afternoon at two o'clock. I think that's when the services start, isn't it? Two o'clock tomorrow afternoon. And the prayer cards will be given here at the afternoon service. But now, when you get a card, come back so you can be prayed for, because tomorrow night they'll be called.
And then we're going to try with the grace of God now, feeling that we found favor with Him, to place these other nights in praying for the sick, everybody. We got about two or three hundred prayer cards, they tell me, out here tonight.
text_no:
Hvis det er noen her som ikke forstår engelsk, håper jeg at noen ved siden av dem kan oversette. I morgen ettermiddag klokken to starter møtene, ikke sant? Klokken to i morgen ettermiddag. Bønnkø-kortene vil deles ut her på ettermiddagsmøtet. Men når du får et kort, kom tilbake slik at du kan bli bedt for, fordi de vil bli kalt opp i morgen kveld.
Med Guds nåde skal vi forsøke å vie de andre kveldene til å be for de syke. De forteller meg at vi har rundt to eller tre hundre bønnkø-kort her i kveld.

text:
So we want to take our time and just speak to you just a short time in the Word. We're going to speak on now: divine healing, how to accept it. Mr. Rasmussen will be giving lectures on that: how to accept your healing.
And now, tonight I wish to read for just a little text, found in Saint Luke the 19th chapter, and the 42nd verse.
And Jesus said unto him, Receive thy sight: thy faith has saved thee.
text_no:
Vi ønsker å bruke litt tid på å tale til dere om Ordet. Vi skal nå snakke om guddommelig helbredelse, og hvordan man kan ta imot den. Herr Rasmussen vil holde forelesninger om dette: hvordan man kan ta imot sin helbredelse.
I kveld vil jeg lese en kort tekst fra Evangeliet etter Lukas, kapittel 19, vers 42: 
Og Jesus sa til ham: «Bli seende! Din tro har frelst deg.»

text:
You notice that name there, the way that's written. "Thy faith has saved thee." Now, there's scholars here; I'm not. But when I find a word, I go back to my Greek, to look it up. Now, that word in the Greek is "Sozo." So the word "save" for salvation is Sozo. Same one for healing. Sozo means that they were physically saved, or spiritually saved.
Both word applies because it was by the same atonement, the same man on the same day. "He was wounded for our transgressions, with His stripes, we were healed," see.
So our scene opens tonight… And just for a short time, so we can have time to run the prayer line through. Our scene opens at the south gate of Jericho. And to the one that we're looking at, is a beggar. He had become quite middle-aged man or a little better. And let's just turn our thoughts to him for a few moments, and learn by his action what we should do tonight when we come to the line.
text_no:
Merk deg navnet der, slik det er skrevet: "Din tro har frelst deg." Nå er det lærde her; jeg er ikke én av dem. Men når jeg finner et ord, går jeg tilbake til min greske oversettelse for å undersøke det. Det greske ordet for "frelse" er "Sozo." Så ordet "frelse" for frelse er Sozo. Det samme ordet brukes for helbredelse. Sozo betyr at de ble fysisk frelst eller åndelig frelst. 
Begge betydninger gjelder fordi det var ved samme forsoning, samme mann på samme dag. "Han ble såret for våre overtredelser, ved Hans sår ble vi helbredet," se.
Scenen åpner seg i kveld… Og for en kort stund, slik at vi får tid til å ha bønnekøen. Vår scene åpner ved sørporten i Jeriko. Personen vi ser på, er en tigger. Han hadde blitt ganske middelaldrende, eller litt eldre. La oss rette tankene mot ham i noen minutter, og lære av hans handlinger hva vi bør gjøre i kveld når vi kommer til køen.

text:
Now, it was in a day that when the Jews were all under heavy taxation from the Roman government, and times were very hard. And there were many beggars in the city and in Jerusalem and all of Judea, many afflicted people who could not make their livelihood in the fields working. They were lame, blind, lepers. Oh, such a scene.
I wonder if there'd be anybody in here ever seen leprosy. Let's see your hands if there has been. I don't even see a hand that's ever seen leprosy. Well, I've hugged lepers in my arms. And I used to think that it was contagious, but it isn't. Leprosy is caused by a insufficient diet. And it is not contagious.
I've had lepers with no hands, ears eat off, just stand and hug them. And they'd weep. Down into Africa, into India, and different places where they have so much leprosy.
text_no:
På den tiden var jødene under tung beskatning fra den romerske regjeringen, og det var vanskelige tider. Det var mange tiggere i byen, i Jerusalem og hele Judea—mange lidende mennesker som ikke kunne tjene til livets opphold ved å arbeide i markene. De var lamme, blinde, spedalske. Å, for et syn.
Jeg lurer på om noen her inne noen gang har sett spedalskhet. La oss se hendene deres hvis noen har. Jeg ser ingen hender. Vel, jeg har holdt spedalske i armene mine. Jeg pleide å tro at det var smittsomt, men det er det ikke. Spedalskhet skyldes en utilstrekkelig diett og er ikke smittsom.
Jeg har møtt spedalske uten hender, med avspiste ører, og stått og klemt dem. De gråt. Dette var i Afrika, India og ulike steder hvor spedalskhet er utbredt.

text:
And I was watching a man one day when we was in Bombay. The American people had given me some money to give to the poor. And I just love to do those things. And I'd change it into rupees. And I started out into the street to give it away to the beggars, the poor people, and they almost had to get the militia to get me back in again, just running over me.
And all night when they learned I had some money and was there in the Taj Motel … or hotel (That's at the gates of India.), I could hear them hollering, "Padre! Padre!" all night. Now, it's not an Indian word. Padre really means "father" in Spanish. But Spanish is spoke quite a bit in there.
text_no:
Jeg husker en dag i Bombay da jeg så en mann. Noen amerikanere hadde gitt meg penger til å gi til de fattige, noe jeg virkelig elsker å gjøre. Jeg vekslet pengene til rupees og gikk ut i gatene for å dele dem ut til tiggerne og de fattige. Det ble nesten nødvendig å hente militæret for å få meg tilbake, så mange prøvde å nå meg.
Gjennom hele natten, etter at folk fikk vite at jeg hadde penger og bodde på Taj Hotel (ved portene til India), kunne jeg høre dem rope "Padre! Padre!" Det er egentlig ikke et indisk ord; "Padre" betyr "far" på spansk, men spansk snakkes ganske mye der.

text:
And they was all night… I couldn't eat. I'd go to the window. And I want to ask you Canadian people that see how grateful you are to God for your wonderful nation here.
And there they were laying in the streets. Little babies with their little bellies swelled up like that, dying, starved to death. Little mothers holding their little babies, and their face and jaws all sunk in. Men coming down the streets, nothing but a loincloth on, little old half-starved monkey following behind him. They laid on the street like cordwood.
And when they would die over the night, they would pick them up, go down to a great salamander. No John 14 ceremony; just dump them in. They don't know who they are or where they come from. They just died on the street, they're beggars, and that's all.
You don't know how to appreciate a real nation like you live in. That's right. You Canadian [Blank spot on tape.] And I think in the face of that we ought to try to hold these nations by prayer, doing what we can to keep this the way it is.
text_no:
De var der hele natten... Jeg kunne ikke spise. Jeg gikk til vinduet. Jeg vil spørre dere, canadiske folk, om å merke hvor takknemlige dere er til Gud for deres fantastiske nasjon her.
De lå i gatene. Små babyer med oppblåste mager, døende av sult. Unge mødre holdt sine babyer, med innsunkne ansikter og kjever. Menn gikk nedover gatene, iført kun lendekleder, med halvstjålne apekatter etter seg. De lå i gatene som vedstabler.
Når de døde om natten, ble de plukket opp og kastet i en stor ovn. Ingen seremoni som i Johannes 14; de bare dumpet dem inn. Ingen visste hvem de var eller hvor de kom fra. De bare døde i gatene, de var tiggere, og det var alt.
Dere vet ikke hvordan dere skal sette pris på en virkelig nasjon som den dere lever i. Det er riktig. Dere kanadiere [Tomt område på lydbånd.] Jeg tror at vi i møte med dette skal forsøke å holde våre nasjoner i bønn, og gjøre det vi kan for å bevare det slik det er.

text:
Oh, what a pitiful sight to see lepers and beggars. And to what I was trying to get at: There was a man running around there. We would take these rupees and throw them out the window to them. And when they did they'd… There was a fellow there that didn't have any arms; leprosy had eat him off, up on his arms. And it gets kind of white like warts, and it just drops off, their arms and what more.
And I said to Billy, my boy, I said, "How could that fellow ever pick up a rupee?" So he went down to watch him at the door. He went out, went down to another part of the hotel. Got to throwing the rupees out down there for them. And as soon as they could get them, they'd take around the corner where they could get some curry.
text_no:
Det er hjerteskjærende å se spedalske og tiggere. Det jeg prøver å fortelle om er en mann som løp rundt der. Vi kastet noen rupeer ut av vinduet til dem. Blant dem var en mann som manglet armer; spedalskhet hadde spist dem bort. Hans armer var dekket av hvite, vorte-lignende flekker før de bare falt av.
Jeg sa til min sønn Billy: "Hvordan kan den mannen plukke opp en rupi?" Billy gikk ned for å se på ham ved døren. Han gikk ut og ned til en annen del av hotellet. Der begynte han å kaste rupeer ut til dem. Så snart de fikk tak i dem, gikk de rundt hjørnet for å kjøpe seg noe karri.

text:
Now, a rupee is twenty-four cents. And it would keep them for two or three days, on twenty-four cents. Costs about three cents to get a little plate of curry. And what it was, sheep feet boiled up with the hair still on it, and so forth and little ri… Oh, it was terrible. Go through the street with your fingers on your nose.
And then the slave markets where they took young women there. Just bring young, beautiful women up and auction them off like you would a cow or anything else. You don't know what it means to live in a nation that still has respect for God.
text_no:
En rupi tilsvarer tjuefire cent og kunne forsørge dem i to eller tre dager med tjuefire cent. Det koster omkring tre cent å få et lite fat med curry. Det bestod av for eksempel saueføtter kokt med hårene på og litt ris. Å, det var fryktelig. Man måtte gå gjennom gaten med fingrene på nesen.
Deretter var det slavemarkedene hvor unge kvinner ble tatt. Man bare førte frem unge, vakre kvinner og auksjonerte dem bort som om de var kyr eller noe annet. Du vet ikke hva det betyr å leve i en nasjon som fortsatt har respekt for Gud.

text:
And Billy got these fellows all down that way, all the rest of them. And I motioned to that man with no hands, and I throwed a rupee out when they wasn't noticing, and it circled to the ground. You know how he got it up? Licked it up with his tongue on that dirty, filthy street. And around the corner he went, screaming at the top of his voice, to get him something to eat.
And think, you raked out your garbage can tonight, enough to feed them. That's right. We're just not grateful enough.
And in the day that we read of tonight, it was like scenes like that, that our Lord saw. And then, a beggar in these special days when taxation and so forth was so high, when a man came out to go to his work, he could perhaps just afford one coin a day. And the first beggar that he come in contact with, well, that was the lucky one. He couldn't give, maybe, two coins a day. And there were hundreds of beggars.
text_no:
Billy fikk med seg alle til den delen av området, alle de andre også. Jeg vinket til mannen uten hender og kastet en rupee da de ikke la merke til det, og den dalte til bakken. Vet du hvordan han plukket den opp? Han slikket den opp med tungen fra den skitne, møkkete gaten. Deretter løp han rundt hjørnet, skrikende høyt for å få seg noe å spise.
Tenk på det; avfallet du kastet i søppelbøtten i kveld, kunne vært nok til å mette dem. Det stemmer. Vi er bare ikke takknemlige nok.
I dagen vi leste om i kveld, var det slike scener vår Herre så. I disse spesielle tidene da beskatningen var så høy, kunne en mann som gikk ut for å arbeide kanskje bare avse én mynt om dagen. Den første tiggeren han møtte, var den heldige. Han kunne kanskje ikke gi to mynter om dagen, og det var hundrevis av tiggere.

text:
And let's imagine tonight that our character that we're speaking of, Bartimaeus, that he had been standing, and it'd been a bad morning. He hadn't been able to find anyone who could give him a coin. And it was a cold October morning, so his ragged coat he had wrapped around him, he just kind of shivered a little and felt his way back till he hit the wall. And he sat down by the side of the wall.
And in the warm sun as it begin to warm him, he begin to think, study. Do you like to do that? I do. Just get away to yourself somewhere, sit down, think it all over.
text_no:
La oss forestille oss i kveld at vår karakter, Bartimeus, hadde en dårlig morgen. Han sto der og hadde ikke funnet noen som kunne gi ham en mynt. Det var en kald oktobermorgen, og han hadde surret sin fillete frakk rundt seg. Han skalv litt og famlet seg tilbake til han støtte på en vegg. Han satte seg ned ved siden av veggen.
Da sola begynte å varme ham, begynte han å tenke og reflektere. Liker du å gjøre det? Jeg gjør. Bare komme seg bort alene et sted, sette seg ned og tenke det hele gjennom.

text:
And while he was sitting there thinking, he hears a little clipping of the hooves coming down the street, old cobblestone street that runs by there, had been there for many, many years. And this little clipping, he knew it was someone approaching, so he raises up and he said, "Please give to me a coin this morning. I am blind."
And he almost staggers right into the little mule. And the one on the mule said, "One side, beggar. I am on my road into the city. And I am the servant of the Lord. And there is coming a fanatic to this city today. And we must get with the council of the churches and stop this stuff before it ever gets into the city. And I'm on my road now to see that this is stopped. I have no time for a beggar, so step to one side."
text_no:
Mens han satt der og tenkte, hører han en liten klapring av hover som kommer nedover den gamle brosteinsgaten, som hadde vært der i mange, mange år. Denne klapringen visste han betydde at noen nærmet seg. Han reiste seg og sa: "Vær så snill og gi meg en mynt i dag. Jeg er blind."
Han snubler nesten rett inn i den lille muldyren. Personen på muldyren sa: "Flytt deg, tigger. Jeg er på vei inn til byen og er Herrens tjener. Det kommer en fanatiker til denne byen i dag, og vi må møte menighetsrådet og stanse dette før det får fotfeste i byen. Jeg har ingen tid for en tigger, så rykk til side."

text:
Find out he was a priest. And he said, "Oh, servant of the Lord," said the blind man, "I would not interfere with you on your commission to God. I am sorry I stopped you. But what is this fanatic that's a coming?"
"Oh, it's that man who's a divine healer up there, the Galilean prophet, He calls Himself. And He's on His road here, and I'm the head of the ministerial association, so I have to see that none of that stuff gets scattered in our city, around here. So I'm on my way. One side, beggar." And on he goes.
Then he moves back over to the warm sunshine and sits down again. And there he begins to get a new thought. A Galilean prophet is to come to this town, a healer. Well, then he begins to dream. You know, his mind goes back to many years previous to that when he was a little black-headed Jewish boy. Used to play out on the hillsides of the Jordan river.
text_no:
Han fikk vite at mannen var en prest. Presten sa da, "Å, Herrens tjener," sa den blinde mannen, "jeg ville ikke forstyrre deg i din tjeneste for Gud. Jeg beklager at jeg stoppet deg. Men hva er det for en fanatiker som kommer hit?"
"Å, det er den mannen som kaller seg en guddommelig helbreder, profeten fra Galilea. Han påstår at Han er det. Og Han er på vei hit, og jeg er leder for presteforeningen, så jeg må sørge for at ingen av Hans lærdommer spres i vår by. Så jeg er på vei. Flytt deg, tigger." Så går han videre.
Den blinde flytter seg tilbake til det varme solskinnet og setter seg ned igjen. Der begynner han å få en ny tanke. En profet fra Galilea skal komme til byen, en helbreder. Han begynner å drømme. Minnene går tilbake til mange år tidligere, da han var en liten mørkhåret jødisk gutt. Han pleide å leke på åssidene ved Jordan-elven.

text:
And one of his greatest delights was to go in in the afternoon before his nap time, and let his mother get him up in her arms. And how he used to look at those big, soft eyes of his mother as she stroked his little black hair back out of his eyes, and begin to tell him Bible stories.
You know there's something about bringing up a child in the way that it should go. Teach it the right thing, and when it's old, it'll not depart from it.
And he used to remember the stories that his mother told him of the great mighty Jehovah. How Jehovah used to blast forth His voice in a time of trial and change the situation.
text_no:
En av hans største gleder var å gå inn på ettermiddagen før lur-tid, og la moren hans løfte ham opp i armene. Han elsket å se inn i de store, myke øynene hennes når hun strøk det lille svarte håret vekk fra øynene hans og begynte å fortelle ham bibelhistorier.
Det er noe spesielt med å oppdra et barn på riktig måte. Lær det det rette, og når det blir gammelt, vil det ikke vike fra det.
Han husket historiene moren fortalte ham om den store, mektige Jehova. Hvordan Jehova pleide å løfte fram Sin stemme i tider med prøvelser og endre situasjonen.

text: How that he used to love to hear especially that story of Elisha, the prophet. And how that Elisha went in and laid his body upon the body of a little dead baby. And that prophet was so anointed until the little baby come to life again. Oh, that just thrilled his heart.
text_no: Han elsket spesielt å høre historien om profeten Elisa. Elisa la sin kropp på en liten død baby, og profeten var så salvet at babyen kom til live igjen. Det begeistret ham dypt.

text:
And it thrills my heart. I can think of the story when the Shunammite woman had been kind to that man of God. She didn't do it to receive anything, but you just cannot help from receiving a reward when you're good to God's children. "Verily, verily, I say unto you, if you shall give one of these little ones as much as a cup of water, you will in nowise lose your reward."
So be kind to one another. If the meal barrel is empty over to that house, take them something over there. Be kind. If the widow needs some coal, buy her some. God will bless you for it.
text_no:
Det gleder mitt hjerte. Jeg tenker på historien om kvinnen fra Shunem som var vennlig mot Guds mann. Hun gjorde det ikke for å få noe tilbake, men du kan ikke unngå å motta en belønning når du er god mot Guds barn. "Sannelig, sannelig, sier Jeg dere: Hvis dere gir så mye som et glass vann til en av disse små, skal dere ingenlunde miste deres lønn."
Vær derfor gode mot hverandre. Hvis matkrukken er tom i et hus, ta med noe dit. Vær hjelpsomme. Hvis en enke trenger kull, kjøp det for henne. Gud vil velsigne dere for det.

text:
And so, showing all the kindness, the Shunammite woman had built a little side room for the prophet to lay in. And the prophet said, "Go, see if she wants me to speak to the king for her."
She said, "No." She dwelt among her people and everything was all right.
But Gehazi said, "She has no children." And Elisha blessed her, prayed for her, and she received a child. When it was about twelve years old, I think the little fellow must have had a sunstroke. He was out in the field with his daddy in time of harvest, and he begin to scream, "My head, my head!"
And a young man carried him to his mama. She sat him on her lap, and along about noon, he died. And now, aside from our text for a moment, I want you to watch the gallant faith of that woman. She took the little baby and laid him on Elisha's bed. That's a good place to put him: on the bed where the prophet had laid, where a house, where a home, where prayer has been made. That's the place to go.
text_no:
Den vennlige kvinnen fra Shunem bygde et lite siderom til profeten slik at han kunne hvile der. Profeten sa: "Gå og se om hun ønsker at jeg skal tale til kongen for henne." Hun svarte: "Nei." Hun bodde blant sitt folk og alt var i orden.
Men Gehazi sa: "Hun har ingen barn." Da velsignet Elisha henne og ba for henne, og hun fikk et barn. Da barnet var omtrent tolv år gammelt, tror jeg han fikk et solstikk. Han var ute i åkeren med sin far under innhøstingen og ropte: "Hodet mitt, hodet mitt!"
En ung mann bar ham til moren. Hun satte ham på fanget, og ved middagstid døde han. Nå, bort fra vår tekst et øyeblikk, vil jeg at dere skal legge merke til kvinnenes store tro. Hun tok barnet og la ham på Elishas seng. Det var et godt sted å legge ham: på sengen hvor profeten hadde hvilt, i et hjem hvor bønn hadde blitt utført. Det er stedet å gå.

text:
And she said to her servant, "Saddle me one of those mules, and don't you stop lest I bid you, and go straight to the prophet." Now, she knew that if she could get to the prophet, she'd find out why her baby had died.
Well, God don't always tell His prophets everything that He does. He just tells His prophets what He wants them to know.
So on the road over Elisha looked out of his cave and he said, "Here comes the Shunammite. And she's got sorrow in her heart. And God has kept it from me. I don't know what's wrong with her." So when she got close, he said, "Is all well with thee? All well with thy husband? Is all well with the baby?"
Now, listen, mother, that woman with her baby laying dead in the room, she said, "All is well!" I love that, see. Elisha was God's representative on the earth at that time. And she knew if she could get to God's servant, she might… She wasn't thinking of her baby back, she had no idea of it. But she knew she would probably find out: This man could ask God and God would talk to him and tell why they took the baby.
text_no:
Hun sa til sin tjener: "Sal opp en av de muldyrene, og stopp ikke med mindre jeg ber deg om det, og gå rett til profeten." Hun visste at hvis hun kunne nå frem til profeten, ville hun finne ut hvorfor babyen hennes hadde dødd.
Gud forteller ikke alltid sine profeter alt Han gjør. Han forteller dem kun det Han vil at de skal vite. På veien så Elisja ut av hulen sin og sa: "Her kommer kvinnen fra Sjunem. Hun har sorg i hjertet sitt, og Gud har holdt det skjult for meg. Jeg vet ikke hva som er galt med henne." Da hun kom nærmere, spurte han: "Står alt bra til med deg? Står alt bra til med mannen din? Står alt bra til med barnet?"
Hør her, mor: Kvinnen, med babyen sin liggende død i rommet, svarte: "Alt står bra til!" Jeg elsker det, ser du. Elisja var Guds representant på jorden på den tiden. Hun visste at hvis hun kunne nå Guds tjener, kunne hun kanskje… Hun tenkte ikke på å få babyen tilbake, hun hadde ingen anelse om det. Men hun visste at han kunne be Gud og Gud ville fortelle hvorfor barnet ble tatt.

text:
And she said, "All is well!" And then she begin to reveal to him what had happened.
Now, Elisha said, "You take this staff and run before me and lay it on the baby."
I've said the other night, I believe that's where Paul got laying on handkerchiefs. Elisha knew that what he had touched was blessed. He was anointed with the Holy Spirit. That's the reason we lay hands on the sick.
text_no:
Hun sa: "Alt er bra!" Deretter begynte hun å fortelle ham hva som hadde skjedd.
Elisha sa da: "Ta denne staven og løp foran meg, og legg den på barnet."
Jeg nevnte forleden kveld at jeg tror det er her Paulus fikk idéen om å legge hendene på lommetørklær. Elisha visste at det han hadde berørt, var velsignet. Han var salvet med Den Hellige Ånd. Det er derfor vi legger hendene på de syke.

text:
Now, but what happened? The woman's faith was not in the staff. It was in the prophet. So she said, "As the Lord liveth, and your soul liveth, I will not leave you." So there wasn't but one thing for the prophet to do, that was gird up his loins, get started with her.
I like that. Hang on to it. When you're satisfied you're right, stay with it.
And the prophet went on. And the boy took the stick and laid it on the child, but no life. He met them coming back. Elisha walked into the room, not knowing what to do. But he walked back and forth, up and down in the room, praying. And went and laid his body on that little dead baby, his lips against its lips, and his nose against its nose. And the baby sneezed seven times and come to life.
Oh, how little Bartimaeus loved that story. You like that story, you little blond-headed girl sitting here? All right. That's a beautiful story. And that's what God is yet today. You believe that, little boy sitting there? You do? That's good. Oh, them is nice stories. And they're the truth.
And little Bartimaeus how he loved that story. But, oh, you know what? The preacher said that those days were all passed. God didn't do that no more. But how wrong those priests were. God never changes. He's just the same all the time. Never changes. He's still almighty God. And if He can't do all things, then He's not almighty God. And if He is almighty God, He can do all things.
text_no:
Hva skjedde så? Kvinnens tro var ikke på staven, men på profeten. Hun sa: "Så sant Herren lever, og din sjel lever, jeg vil ikke forlate deg." Det var bare én ting for profeten å gjøre: å feste beltet og dra med henne.
Jeg liker det. Hold fast ved det. Når du er overbevist om at du har rett, stå ved det.
Profeten fulgte med. Gutten tok staven og la den på barnet, men det var ikke noe liv. Da de møtte dem på vei tilbake, gikk Elisa inn i rommet uten å vite hva han skulle gjøre. Han vandret frem og tilbake i rommet og ba. Deretter la han kroppen sin over den døde babyen, hans lepper mot dens lepper og hans nese mot dens nese. Og babyen nøs syv ganger og kom til liv.
Åh, hvor lille Bartimaeus elsket den historien. Liker du den historien, du lille blondhårede jente som sitter her? Bra. Det er en vakker historie. Og det er slik Gud er i dag også. Tror du det, lille gutt som sitter der? Gjør du det? Det er flott. Disse historiene er fine, og de er sanne.
Lille Bartimaeus elsket den historien. Men vet du hva? Forkynneren sa at de dagene var forbi. At Gud ikke gjorde slike ting lenger. Men hvor feil de prestene tok. Gud forandrer seg aldri. Han er den samme hele tiden. Endrer seg aldri. Han er fortsatt allmektig Gud. Og hvis Han ikke kan gjøre alle ting, er Han ikke allmektig Gud. Men hvis Han er allmektig Gud, kan Han gjøre alle ting.

text:
So then, little Bartimaeus happened to think then of another story that he liked real well. And not over five hundred yards from where he was sitting, one time the great Jordan, in the month of April, when the Judean hills, the snow had melted, all the fields were flooded with water. The greatest time the floods, the great angry muddy waves a rolling down from the mountains of Judea, down through the plains. And the Jordan had swelled way out, and it was many times its size.
But just at that time Israel arrived on the other side. You see, God gave them a promise on this side. They were on their road to claim that promise. And everything that stands in the way of God's promise has to give a way. No matter whether it's a worse time, or the worse kind of sickness, or how muddy black it looks, everything gives a way to God's promise. Certainly.
text_no:
Så, den lille Bartimeus kom til å tenke på en annen fortelling som han likte veldig godt. Ikke mer enn fem hundre meter fra der han satt, en gang i april, da snøen i Judeas fjell hadde smeltet og alle markene var oversvømte. Flommen var på sitt høyeste, og de store, rasende, gjørmete bølgene rullet ned fra Judeas fjell og gjennom slettene. Jordan-elven hadde vokst seg betydelig større enn vanlig.
Akkurat på den tiden ankom Israel på den andre siden. Gud hadde gitt dem et løfte på denne siden, og de var på vei for å gjøre krav på det løftet. Alt som står i veien for Guds løfte må vike. Uansett om det er de verste tidene, den verste sykdommen, eller hvor mørkt og gjørmete det ser ut, alt må vike for Guds løfte. Helt sikkert.

text:
And how he loved to hear his mother tell how the lightning roared, and that big Jordan waved back, and held her peace until two million Israelites crossed over on foot. Oh, that made his little heart… He'd almost go to sleep, and he'd shake his head and say, "Mama, tell me another story."
Then he remembered too that not standing a hundred and fifty yards from where he was sitting, a great thing happened. And right, not twenty yards from where he was sitting, down that same road, over those same old cobblestones, about four hundred years previous to that, the great mighty prophet Elijah and Elisha come down that road arm in arm, going towards Jordan to make her open again.
text_no:
Og hvor glad han var i å høre sin mor fortelle hvordan lynet brølte, og hvordan den store Jordan delte seg og sto stille til to millioner israelitter kunne gå over til fots. Å, det fikk det lille hjertet hans til å... Han nesten sovnet, men ristet på hodet og sa, "Mamma, fortell meg en historie til."
Han husket også at ikke mer enn hundre og femti meter fra der han satt, hadde noe stort skjedd. Og rett, ikke mer enn tjue meter fra der han satt, ned den samme veien, over de samme gamle brosteinene, hadde, omtrent fire hundre år tidligere, de store mektige profetene Elia og Elisa kom ned den veien arm i arm, på vei mot Jordan for å få henne til å åpne seg igjen.

text:
Why? Would it open again? God's the same. And so he thought, "Oh, if I could have only lived in those days. If I could just be sitting here blind." Then he scratched his head, and said, "What am I thinking these things, since that priest said about that Galilean prophet. But somehow or another, I'm beginning to think different. I haven't thought those stories for years." Something was fixing to happen.
And when something in you begins to desire, and seek out for God, something's fixing to happen. God knows how to get everything set up for His drama.
Then we notice that as he begin to think, "If I would've been sitting here when Elisha…" God usually has just one major prophet at a time. And Elijah was going down to the river to put his mantle, his coat, upon Elisha. And he was become prophet because Elijah was going up.
text_no:
Hvorfor? Ville det åpne seg igjen? Gud er den samme. Han tenkte: "Åh, om jeg bare hadde levd i de dagene. Om jeg bare kunne sitte her blind." Så klødde han seg i hodet og sa: "Hva tenker jeg på, etter hva den presten sa om den galske profeten. Men på en eller annen måte begynner jeg å tenke annerledes. Jeg har ikke tenkt på disse historiene på årevis." Noe var i ferd med å skje.
Når noe i deg begynner å lengte etter og søke Gud, er noe i ferd med å skje. Gud vet hvordan Han skal forberede alt for Sin dramatiske plan.
Vi legger merke til at han begynner å tenke: "Hvis jeg hadde sittet her da Elisha..." Gud har vanligvis bare én hovedprofet om gangen. Elia var på vei ned til elven for å legge sin kappe, sin frakk, over Elisha. Elisha skulle bli profet fordi Elia skulle gå opp.

text:
And as they walked down by that gate, side by side, he said, "If I could have only lived in that day, I would've rose up from this place where I'm sitting. And I'd've rushed out into the street and said, 'Oh, prophets of God, lay your hands on me. And I believe if you'll pray for me, God will restore my sight.'"
But alas, the days of miracles was passed to him. The priest told him there was no such a thing as that happen anymore. Elijah had been gone for years, and Elisha had been dead for years. And oh, Jehovah had forgot the people. But He doesn't forget the people. He still remembers.
Then he remembered just out from that gate, that great Joshua had marched Israel right over those same cobblestones, right around the walls of Jericho. And all of Jericho was shut up. Although they had the Israelite outnumbered many times, but the fear of the Lord went before them. They held their peace.
text_no:
Mens de gikk ned ved porten, side ved side, sa han: "Hvis jeg bare kunne ha levd i den tiden, ville jeg ha reist meg fra der jeg sitter nå. Jeg ville ha løpt ut i gaten og sagt: 'Å, Guds profeter, legg hendene på meg. Og jeg tror at hvis dere ber for meg, vil Gud gjenopprette synet mitt.'"
Men akk, for ham var miraklenes tid forbi. Presten hadde fortalt ham at slike ting ikke skjedde lenger. Elia hadde vært borte i årevis, og Elisja hadde vært død i mange år. Å, Jehova hadde glemt folket. Men Han glemmer ikke folket. Han husker dem fortsatt.
Så husket han at like utenfor den porten hadde den store Josva ført Israel over de samme brosteinene, rett rundt murene i Jeriko. Hele Jeriko var stengt. Selv om de utallige ganger overgikk israelittene i antall, gikk frykten for Herren foran dem. De holdt fred.

text:
One day when the great Joshua, not very far from that same wall, was strolling along one afternoon, and he looked, standing there on the side of a little bank, there stood a warrior with his sword pulled. And Joshua pulled his sword, and he run to meet him. He said, "Are you for us, or are you for our enemy?"
And the stranger waved the sword in the air, said, "Nay, but I am the Captain of the host of the Lord." The mighty Joshua threw down his sword, took off his helmet, and ran and fell at His feet. Little did blind Bartimaeus know that less than two hundred yards from there, stood that same Captain of the host of the Lord.
And if the people in this exhibition room tonight here, would only realize that that same Captain of the host of the Lord stands here tonight!
text_no:
En dag da den store Josva, ikke langt fra den samme muren, vandret en ettermiddag og så en kriger stående på siden av en liten bank, trakk han sitt sverd. Josva trakk sitt eget sverd og løp for å møte ham. Han sa: "Er du for oss eller for vår fiende?"
Den fremmede svingte sitt sverd i luften og sa: "Nei, men jeg er hærføreren for Herrens hær." Den mektige Josva kastet sitt sverd, tok av hjelmen og løp for å falle ved Hans føtter. Lite visste den blinde Bartimeus at mindre enn to hundre meter derfra stod den samme hærføreren for Herrens hær.
Og om folket i dette rommet i kveld bare ville innse at den samme hærføreren for Herrens hær står her i kveld!

text:
But he had been told that those days were gone. After awhile his dream is interrupted. Some little children run by, said, "Did you get to see Him? Did you get to see Him?" They were wondering what it was all about. Here come some ladies. And they were talking. "Oh, oh, wasn't it wonderful?"
And Bartimaeus begin to ask, "What's going on?" Just around the corner he heard a lot of noise. You know somehow or another where Jesus is, there's usually a little noise going on. In the Old Testament the only way that they could tell the high priest was alive, when he went behind the Holiest of holies, (He might have been slain by the Lord.), but he had a garment, and at the end of the garment, he had a pomegranate and a bell, a pomegranate and a bell. And when he had to walk a certain way…
And a man that wears a robe of God has a certain walk he has to walk. And as he walked, them bells played, "Holy, holy, holy unto the Lord. Holy, holy, holy unto the Lord." And the only way the congregation could tell whether he was alive or not, they could hear a noise.
text_no:
Han hadde fått høre at de dagene var forbi. Etter en stund ble drømmen hans avbrutt. Noen små barn løp forbi og ropte: "Så du Ham? Fikk du se Ham?" De lurte på hva det hele handlet om. Der kom noen damer gående, og de snakket sammen. "Å, å, var det ikke vidunderlig?"
Bartimeus begynte å spørre: "Hva skjer?" Rett rundt hjørnet hørte han mye støy. Du vet, der Jesus er, pleier det ofte å være litt lyd. I Det gamle testamentet kunne de bare vite at ypperstepresten var i live når han gikk inn i Det aller helligste. (Han kunne ha blitt slått ned av Herren.) Men han hadde en kappe, og i enden av kappen var det en granateple og en bjelle, en granateple og en bjelle. Og han måtte gå på en bestemt måte...
En mann som bærer Guds kappe, må ha en bestemt måte å gå på. Og når han gikk, spilte bjellene: "Hellig, hellig, hellig er Herren. Hellig, hellig, hellig er Herren." Den eneste måten menigheten kunne vite om han var i live, var at de kunne høre lyden.

text:
I sometimes wonder when I go in some of these old cold morgues called churches, icicles hanging down, spiritually speaking, the thermometer forty below zero, I just wonder if there's anything in there alive.
It was like my little brother. I caught a snapping turtle, and I cut his head off. And anyone knows a snapping turtle just keeps on snapping if his head is cut off. And it was laying up on the bank, and my little brother come along and said, "What'd you catch, Billy?"
I said, "I got a turtle."
He said, "Is that him?"
Said, "Yeah."
"Where's his head?"
I said, "Laying up there on the bank."
text_no:
Noen ganger lurer jeg på om det finnes noe levende i disse gamle, kalde likhusene som kalles menigheter. Åndelig sett henger istappene ned, og temperaturen er førti grader under null. 
Det minner meg om en episode med min lille bror. Jeg fanget en snappskilpadde og kuttet av hodet dens. Alle vet at en snappskilpadde fortsetter å bite selv etter at hodet er kuttet av. Den lå på elvebredden, og min lille bror kom forbi og spurte: "Hva har du fanget, Billy?"
Jeg svarte: "Jeg fanget en skilpadde."
Han spurte: "Er det den der?"
Jeg svarte: "Ja."
"Men hvor er hodet?"
Jeg sa: "Det ligger der borte på bredden."

text:
And he went up there, and he started to take a stick to turn it over, and it snapped the stick. He said, "Hey, I thought you said he was dead."
Well, I said, "I separated his head from his body. He should be dead."
He scratched his head. He said, "He's just dead and don't know it."
And that's just a whole lot of good theology in that. Oh, brother, when Christ comes, He brings life abundantly. Said, "If they hold their peace, the rocks will immediately cry out," when He was coming to Jerusalem.
And we find out a noise was around the bend. He said, "What's all the noise about?" See, he had been thinking on those things. It had to happen. Have you been thinking on those things for tonight? If you have, it's going to happen. It's just got to. You draw nigh unto God, He will draw nigh unto you.
text_no:
Han gikk opp dit og brukte en pinne for å snu på det, men pinnen knakk. Han sa: "Hei, jeg trodde du sa at han var død."
Jeg svarte: "Jeg skilte hodet fra kroppen hans. Han burde være død."
Han klødde seg i hodet og sa: "Han er bare død og vet det ikke."
Det ligger mye god teologi i det. Bror, når Kristus kommer, bringer Han liv i overflod. Han sa: "Hvis de tier, skal steinene rope," da Han kom til Jerusalem.
Vi finner at det var en lyd rundt svingen. Han spurte: "Hva er all denne støyen?" Se, han hadde tenkt på disse tingene. Det måtte skje. Har du tenkt på disse tingene for i kveld? Hvis du har, vil det skje. Det må bare det. Tred nærmere Gud, så vil Han tre nærmere deg.

text: And then, what took place? After awhile here come a bunch of people singing, "Hosanna in the highest, blessed is He that cometh in the name of the Lord. Blessed is this Galilean prophet, for He makes the blind to see, and the deaf to hear. Blessed be the name of the Lord."
text_no: Og hva skjedde så? Etter en stund kom en gruppe mennesker syngende: "Hosanna i det høyeste, velsignet er Han som kommer i Herrens navn. Velsignet er denne Galileiske profeten, for Han får de blinde til å se og de døve til å høre. Velsignet være Herrens navn."

text:
Then behind that come the critics, them priests and religionists of that day, saying, "Shut up. Hold your peace. There's nothing to that guy." Listen at it. And once and awhile something smashes against the wall. It's an over ripe tomato or something, thrown at Him.
Oh, if he could've only seen, walking right down through that street, a little man, not too big, no beauty we should desire, graying in His hair, walking slowly, not paying any attention. And there was them priests saying, "Say, You holy-roller, You that said You raised Lazarus from the dead. We got a whole graveyard full of them up here. Come raise them. Let us see You do it."
You know them kind of spirits never died. They still live. And the other one does too, thank God.
text_no:
Bak i folkemengden kommer kritikerne, prestene og religionistene fra den tiden, og sier: "Ti stille. Vær stille. Han fyren der er ingenting." Hør på det. Og nå og da smeller noe mot veggen. Det kan være en overmoden tomat eller noe annet, kastet mot Ham.
Åh, hvis de bare kunne se, der Han gikk nedover gaten, en liten mann, ikke særlig stor, uten ytre skjønnhet som tiltrakk oss, med grånende hår, gående sakte og uten å bry seg. Og der var prestene som ropte: "Du hellig-rullende, Du som sa at Du oppreiste Lasarus fra de døde. Vi har en hel gravlund full av dem her oppe. Kom og reis dem opp. La oss se Deg gjøre det."
Disse åndene har aldri dødd. De lever fortsatt. Og den andre ånden gjør det samme, takk Gud.

text:
"Hey, You done miracles up in another place. Let's see You perform a miracle here. We'll believe You. You hypocrite, we know that the days of miracles is past." Bartimaeus got all confused. He said, "What is this anyhow?" People just climbing over the top of him.
After awhile, a gentle little lady said, "Oh, beggar, I'm sorry that they've walked over you. What was you asking, sir?"
Said, "Who is it that passes by that's causing all this commotion, one one way and one another?"
text_no:
"Hei, Du gjorde mirakler et annet sted. La oss se Deg utføre et mirakel her, så skal vi tro på Deg. Du hykler, vi vet at miraklenes tid er forbi." Bartimeus ble helt forvirret. Han sa: "Hva er dette egentlig?" Folk klatret bare over ham.
Etter en stund sa en mild dame: "Å, tigger, jeg beklager at de har gått over deg. Hva var det du spurte om, herr?" 
Han sa: "Hvem er det som passerer og forårsaker all denne oppstandelsen i alle retninger?"

text:
You know, when Jesus is around, it usually produces that kind of a crowd. One for Him, one against Him. One saying it's the devil, and another one saying it's God. And it hasn't changed.
And so he said, "There's something taking place. And I don't understand what it is."
And she said, "Why, sir, have you never learned of Jesus of Nazareth?"
"No, I don't believe I ever heard of Him."
"Well, He's a great prophet out of Galilee. Oh, you should see Him. Why, a woman just touched His garment here a few weeks ago, and He turned around and told her what her trouble was. And there was a little man just on the other side of this wall, climbed up in a sycamore tree this morning to see Him. And his wife told him all about these things, and he said, 'Oh, there's no such a thing as that. Them things don't happen. If that man does that, He's a devil.'"
text_no:
Når Jesus er til stede, fører det vanligvis til en oppdeling av folkemengden. Noen er for Ham, andre mot Ham. Noen sier det er djevelen, mens andre sier det er Gud. Og dette har ikke endret seg.
Han sa: "Det skjer noe. Jeg forstår ikke hva det er."
Hun svarte: "Har De aldri hørt om Jesus fra Nasaret?"
"Nei, jeg tror ikke jeg noen gang har hørt om Ham."
"Hans er en stor profet fra Galilea. De burde se Ham. For noen uker siden rørte en kvinne ved klærne Hans, og Han vendte seg rundt og fortalte henne hva som var i veien med henne. Og i morges klatret en liten mann opp i et morbærtre på den andre siden av denne muren for å se ham. Hans kone hadde fortalt ham alt om disse tingene, men han sa: 'Å, det finnes ikke noe slikt. Slike ting skjer ikke. Hvis den mannen gjør det, er Han en djevel.'"

text:
"So, Zacchaeus climbed up in a sycamore tree and pulled the limbs all around him so nobody would see him. He said, 'I will stand here because I'm a great member of the church. And I'll take this little leaf and raise up here, and He will never see me. And when He passes under this tree, I'll get a good look at Him, and I'll tell the priests all about what I think of Him.'"
"So, when Jesus come around the corner and got under the tree, He stopped. Zacchaeus said, 'Now, wonder what He's going to do.' He looked up in the tree and said, 'Zacchaeus, come down from there. I'm going home with you today for dinner.'" Not only he thought he was hid, but Christ knowed him, and knowed his conditions, and knowed what his name was. He hasn't changed. He's still the same Jesus.
text_no:
Så klatret Sakkeus opp i et morbærtre og skjulte seg blant grenene så ingen skulle se ham. Han sa: «Jeg blir stående her fordi jeg er et fremstående medlem av Menigheten. Jeg tar dette lille bladet og løfter det her, så vil Han aldri se meg. Når Han går under dette treet, skal jeg få et godt blikk på Ham og fortelle prestene alt jeg mener om Ham.»
Da Jesus kom rundt hjørnet og stanset under treet, sa Sakkeus: «Jeg lurer på hva Han skal gjøre nå.» Jesus så opp i treet og sa: «Sakkeus, kom ned derfra. Jeg besøker deg i dag og blir med deg hjem til middag.» Ikke bare trodde Sakkeus at han var skjult, men Kristus kjente ham, visste hans tilstand, og visste hva han het. Han har ikke forandret seg. Han er fortsatt den samme Jesus.

text:
Oh, she begin to tell him. He said, "Well, isn't that the Son of David that's been promised?" Yes, that was enough to set his soul afire. It was his first opportunity. We've had many opportunities through this last week. But that was his first opportunity to ever come in contact with Jesus.
And he raised up. Now, the street runs just about the distance of the end of this arena here, from the wall. And thousands of people swarming around Him, some hollering and screaming and others praising Him. And he screamed, "O Jesus, Thou Son of David, have mercy on me."
text_no:
Hun begynte å fortelle ham. Han sa: "Er det ikke Davids sønn som er blitt lovet?" Ja, det var nok til å sette hans sjel i brann. Det var hans første mulighet. Vi har hatt mange muligheter gjennom denne uken. Men dette var hans første sjanse til å komme i kontakt med Jesus.
Han reiste seg. Gaten strakte seg omtrent like langt som slutten av denne arenaen her, fra veggen. Tusenvis av mennesker samlet seg rundt Jesus, noen ropte og skrek mens andre priste Ham. Han skrek: "O Jesus, Du Davids sønn, ha miskunn med meg."

text:
Now, Jesus could not have heard his voice. It was too far back. If you were ever there to look and see where it happened… And another thing, with all that howling mob, He couldn't heard it. But what was it? Jesus stopped and stood still. And He turned around. It wasn't his voice that stopped Him. It was his faith that stopped Him.
An insignificant beggar trying to be pushed back. Jesus stopped and said, "What would you that I would do for you?"
He said, "Lord, that I might receive my sight."
He said, "Receive your sight, for thy faith has saved thee."
And as they go on down the street, I can see old blind Bartimaeus standing there looking for his fingers. He said, "He told me. I take His Word. I believe it." And the mob went on. After awhile he begin to see his fingers. And he begin to follow the Lord rejoicing because he had received his sight.
text_no:
Jesus kunne ikke ha hørt hans stemme. Han var for langt bak. Hvis du noen gang var der og så hvor det skjedde… Og en annen ting, med all den skrikende mobben, kunne Han ikke ha hørt det. Men hva var det? Jesus stoppet og stod stille. Og Han snudde seg. Det var ikke hans stemme som stoppet Ham. Det var hans tro.
En ubetydelig tigger som prøvde å bli dyttet tilbake. Jesus stoppet og sa: "Hva vil du at Jeg skal gjøre for deg?"
Han sa: "Herre, at jeg må få synet mitt igjen."
Jesus sa: "Få ditt syn, for din tro har frelst deg."
Og mens de gikk videre nedover gaten, kan jeg se gamle blinde Bartimeus stå der og se på fingrene sine. Han sa: "Han sa det til meg. Jeg tar Hans Ord. Jeg tror det." Og mobben fortsatte. Etter en stund begynte han å se fingrene sine. Og han begynte å følge Herren, jublende fordi han hadde fått synet tilbake.

text:
Sometime ago I read a little story. It may be fiction and it may not, about blind Bartimaeus. They say that he sat by the Jericho gates; he was a married man, had a wife and a little girl. And this little girl at the time of his healing, was perhaps, about ten years old, little curly headed girl, just chubby enough to be lovable.
And he had never seen the little child because he had been blind many years. And they say he had two little turtle doves, and they would do little enchantments, turn over each other, little tumbles, so that it would attract the attention of the tourists.
text_no:
For en tid tilbake leste jeg en liten historie om den blinde Bartimaeus. Den kan være fiksjon eller basert på virkelige hendelser. Det sies at han satt ved portene i Jeriko. Han var gift, hadde en kone og en liten datter. Denne datteren var rundt ti år ved tidspunktet for Bartimaeus' helbredelse. Hun hadde krøllete hår, var litt lubben og veldig kjærlig.
Bartimaeus hadde aldri sett sin lille datter fordi han hadde vært blind i mange år. Det sies også at han hadde to små skilpaddeduer som utførte små triks og rullet over hverandre for å tiltrekke seg oppmerksomhet fra turistene.

text:
If he didn't have something to entertain the tourists, like it is in India and different places with a little monkey or something, the monkey gets a stick and beats the man, and it attracts attention so he can beg.
And blind Bartimaeus' little pigeons turned over each other so that it would attract attention to watch the accuracy of those little doves … or, little doves, making a little flip, jump over on their feet again. And one night, his wife got sick. And the physician could not do anything for her. And he felt his way out along the side of the house. And he said, "O Jehovah, if You will be merciful and spare the life of my wife, I will give You my two turtle doves."
text_no:
Hvis han ikke hadde noe for å underholde turistene, slik som i India og andre steder med en liten ape eller lignende, ville apen få en stokk og slå mannen, noe som tiltrakk oppmerksomhet slik at han kunne tigge.
Blinde Bartimeus' små duer rullet over hverandre for å tiltrekke seg oppmerksomhet og vise deres presise bevegelser, som små hopp over på føttene igjen. En natt ble kona hans syk. Legen kunne ikke gjøre noe for henne. Bartimeus famlet seg langs siden av huset og sa: "O Jehova, hvis Du vil være nådig og spare livet til min kone, vil jeg gi Deg mine to turtelduer."

text:
And his wife got well. So he goes and offers the turtle doves for an offering. Sometime later, he had a lamb. And in those days, the lamb led the man, just like (the blind man), like the dog leads the blind man today. But lambs did it then. They trained the lambs to lead the man. And blind Bartimaeus, how he got back home of a evening, was hook his lamb on his hand, and the lamb would go home and he would lead his way, lead him back to his home.
And his little girl got sick. And she had an awful fever. And the doctor came, and said, "Well, Bartimaeus, I don't think there's anything that I can do for her." She kept getting worse. So, he went out into the night again, knelt down on his knees, and he said, "God, if You'll heal my little girl, I'll give You my lamb."
text_no:
Hans kone ble frisk. Deretter gikk han og ofret turtelduene. En stund senere fikk han et lam. På den tiden ledet lammet mannen, på samme måte som en hund leder en blind i dag. De trente lam til å lede mennesker. Blinde Bartimeus kom hjem om kvelden ved å feste lammet til hånden sin, og lammet førte ham trygt hjem.
Hans lille datter ble syk og fikk høy feber. Legen kom og sa: "Bartimeus, jeg tror ikke det er noe jeg kan gjøre for henne." Tilstanden hennes forverret seg. Så gikk han ut i natten igjen, knelte ned og ba: "Gud, hvis Du helbreder min lille jente, vil jeg gi Deg mitt lam."

text:
And the little girl's fever left. And the next day he was on his road up to sacrifice his lamb. And the priest come out, and he said, "Where goest thou, blind Bartimaeus?"
He said, "Oh, priest of God, I go up to the temple to offer my lamb for a sacrifice." Said, "I promised God that I would do it."
Oh, he said, "Blind Bartimaeus, you can't offer that lamb. I'll give you some money. And you go buy a lamb at the exchange, and take a lamb and offer it. I'll give you the money."
He said, "Thank you kindly, priest of God, but I never promised God a lamb. I promised God this lamb."
Oh, my! There's a lesson in that.
text_no:
Og den lille jentas feber forsvant. Dagen etter var han på vei for å ofre sitt lam. Presten kom ut og sa: "Hvor skal du, blinde Bartimeus?"
Han svarte: "Å, Guds prest, jeg går til tempelet for å ofre mitt lam som et offer. Jeg lovet Gud at jeg skulle gjøre det."
Presten sa: "Blinde Bartimeus, du kan ikke ofre det lammet. Jeg gir deg penger, så kan du kjøpe et lam på markedet og ofre det. Jeg gir deg pengene."
Han svarte: "Tusen takk, Guds prest, men jeg lovet ikke Gud et lam. Jeg lovet Gud dette lammet."
Å, for en lærepenge det er i det.

text:
He said, "You can't give that lamb, Bartimaeus. That lamb is your eyes."
He said, "But, oh, priest of God, if I'll keep my promise to God, God will provide a lamb for Bartimaeus' eyes." That day when he heard that Jesus of Nazareth passed by---watch faith go into action. He grabs his robe and throws it sideways for God had provided a Lamb for blind Bartimaeus' eyes. God's provided Lamb.
And that same Lamb's provided tonight for you, sir, on that cot. That Lamb's provided for you, there in them wheelchairs. That's Lamb's provided for you with heart trouble. That Lamb's provided for you with cancer. He is the same Lamb yesterday, today, and forever. And He's been the One that's led you here tonight for this great service just ready to take forth now.
And if you are a sinner, and don't know Him in the forgiveness of your sins, He's a Lamb provided to take away every blot off of your sinful life.
text_no:
Han sa: "Du kan ikke gi bort det lammet, Bartimeus. Det lammet er dine øyne."
Men Bartimeus svarte: "Å, prest av Gud, hvis jeg holder mitt løfte til Gud, vil Gud sørge for et lam til Bartimeus’ øyne." Den dagen han hørte at Jesus fra Nasaret gikk forbi—se troen komme til liv. Han tar tak i kappen sin og kaster den til siden fordi Gud hadde sørget for et Lam til den blinde Bartimeus' øyne. Guds tilrettelagte Lam.
Og det samme Lammet er tilgjengelig i kveld for deg, herren på den båren. Det Lammet er tilgjengelig for deg, der i rullestolen. Det Lammet er tilgjengelig for deg med hjerteproblem. Det Lammet er tilgjengelig for deg med kreft. Han er det samme Lammet i går, i dag og for alltid. Og Han er Den som har ledet deg hit i kveld for denne store møtet som nå er i ferd med å begynne.
Og hvis du er en synder og ikke kjenner Ham i tilgivelse av dine synder, er Han et Lam gitt for å fjerne alle flekker fra ditt syndige liv.

text:
Let us bow our heads just a moment now, while we think on these things. And if the pianist and organist will give us just a little note, please.
Just before prayer now, I wonder if someone in here who doesn't know this great, marvelous, meek Lamb of God, would say, "God, I want to know that Lamb for the forgiveness of my sins. My soul is spotted. I've made a profession." Or, maybe you have or have not. "But I say by this I'd like to accept Him tonight as the Lamb for my soul, while I hold up my hand." Would you do it? Someone want to be remembered in prayer?
text_no:
La oss bøye hodene våre et øyeblikk mens vi reflekterer over disse tingene. Om pianisten og organisten kan gi oss en liten note, vær så snill.
Rett før vi ber, lurer jeg på om det er noen her som ikke kjenner denne store, vidunderlige, milde Guds Lam, som vil si: "Gud, jeg vil kjenne dette Lammet for tilgivelse av mine synder. Min sjel er plettet. Jeg har gjort en bekjennelse." Eller, kanskje du har eller ikke har. "Men jeg sier med dette at jeg vil akseptere Ham i kveld som Lammet for min sjel, mens jeg løfter opp hånden min." Vil du gjøre det? Er det noen som ønsker å bli husket i bønn?

text:
God bless you here, lady. God bless you, sir, this man sitting here. Someone else would like for that Lamb to lead you to the fountain? God bless you back there, sister. Anyone else on the bottom floor yet? God bless you, my brother over there. That's the way. Believe. If ye believe, God will supply everything you have need of.
Is there another to the balconies to my right, would raise your hand, say, "God be merciful to me. Let the Lamb of God lead me to the fountains of life, of the water of life, tonight. I am thirsty for Christ. I would love to, for Him to lead me." Would you do it? You're blind if you're a sinner. You don't know where you're going, man. If you're a church member, you're still blinded. You're blind by the creed of men.
text_no:
Gud velsigne deg, dame. Gud velsigne deg, herren som sitter her. Er det noen andre som ønsker at Lammet skal lede deg til kilden? Gud velsigne deg der bak, søster. Er det noen andre på første etasje ennå? Gud velsigne deg, min bror der borte. Slik er det. Tro. Hvis du tror, vil Gud gi deg alt du trenger.
Er det noen andre på balkongene til høyre for meg som vil løfte hånden og si: "Gud, vær nådig mot meg. La Guds Lam lede meg til livets kilder, til livets vann, i kveld. Jeg tørster etter Kristus. Jeg ønsker at Han skal lede meg." Vil du gjøre det? Du er blind dersom du er en synder. Du vet ikke hvor du går, menneske. Om du er et menighetsmedlem, er du fortsatt blind. Du er blindet av menneskenes lære.

text:
Is there another on to the right here, to the rear, the balconies to the rear? Quickly. Balconies to my left here would raise your hands? It's just a little thing to do, but you, by your uplifted hand, you'd say, "I … I now want…"
God bless you up there. That's good. Someone else on the bottom floor now that hasn't raised their hand? God bless you back there, sir. God bless you. Whole group of you back there, God be with you. Over here to the right, God be with you. May He lead you tonight to Calvary where there is a fountain filled with blood, drawn from Immanuel's veins, when sinners plunge beneath the flood, lose all their guilty stain.
text_no:
Er det noen andre til høyre her, bakerst, eller på balkongene bak? Raskt nå. Balkongene til venstre her, kan dere rekke opp hånden? Det er en liten ting å gjøre, men ved å løfte hånden, sier dere: "Jeg ... Jeg vil nå..."
Gud velsigne dere oppe der. Det er bra. Er det noen andre på nederste etasje som ikke har løftet hånden? Gud velsigne deg der bak, sir. Gud velsigne deg. Hele gruppen der bak, Gud være med dere. Over her til høyre, Gud være med dere. Måtte Han lede dere i kveld til Golgata, hvor det er en kilde fylt med blod, tappet fra Immanuels årer. Når syndere dykker under floden, mister de alle deres skyldige flekker.

text:
I'm just watching the moving of the Spirit as it's moving in the building, and just taking my time, just letting it really soak in. Do you really, really mean that you love Him? All right. I just seen some hands going up, and I was watching something take place.
Let us pray.
text_no:
Jeg ser Åndens bevegelse i bygningen og tar meg god tid, lar det synke ordentlig inn. Mener dere virkelig at dere elsker Ham? Greit. Jeg så noen hender løftes, og jeg fulgte med på noe som skjedde.
La oss be.

text:
Heavenly Father, may the Lamb of God come quickly now. Lead each of these who raised their hands, to that fountain filled with blood, drawn from Immanuel's veins.
Oh, when I can see Him coming around the wall there at Jericho: His face set towards Calvary, going right on up to Jerusalem to be offered up into the hands of sinful men for a sacrifice, to be a propitiation for our sins and sickness.
And that howling mob, and yet with the sins of the world bearing upon Him, and Him being God Emmanuel. He certainly felt the load that was just before Him. Dark Gethsemane was before Him. The mock trial was before Him. He knew all this and His precious heart was beating high.
text_no:
Himmelske Far, måtte Guds Lam komme raskt nå. Led hver av dem som løftet sine hender, til den fontenen fylt med blod, tappet fra Immanuels årer.
Å, når jeg ser Ham komme rundt muren der ved Jeriko: Hans ansikt satt mot Golgata, rett opp til Jerusalem for å bli overgitt i syndige menneskers hender som et offer, å være en soning for våre synder og sykdommer.
Og den hylende mobben, og likevel med verdens synder på seg, og Han som er Gud Immanuel. Han følte definitivt byrden som lå foran Ham. Mørke Getsemane ventet Ham. Den hånlige rettssaken ventet Ham. Han visste alt dette og Hans dyrebare hjerte banket høyere.

text:
But to think that He wasn't too busy, neither was He too heavy burdened, but what He could stop and have mercy on a blind beggar. You're not too busy tonight, Lord, but what You'll have mercy upon these who raised their hands. Take them to the fountain of water … of the waters of life freely, and may they drink. Thou hast said in Thy Word that He that heareth My Word, and believeth on Him that sent Me, has eternal life, and shall never perish; but is passed from death to life.
I present them to You now, Lord God, in the name of Jesus Christ, as love gifts of the message. Amen.
text_no:
Å tenke at Han ikke var for opptatt eller for tungt belastet til å ha medlidenhet med en blind tigger. Du er ikke for opptatt i kveld, Herre, til å vise barmhjertighet mot de som løftet sine hender. Ta dem til livets kildes vann, og la dem drikke fritt. Du har sagt i Ditt Ord at den som hører Mitt Ord og tror på Ham som har sendt Meg, har evig liv og skal aldri gå fortapt, men er gått over fra døden til livet.
Jeg overgir dem til Deg nå, Herre Gud, i Jesu Kristi navn, som kjærlighetsgaver fra budskapet. Amen.

text:
Let's just sing a little hymn now. This is worship. We had to cut just a little short, but this is a worship. Now, let's get real quiet and sing, "There is a fountain filled with blood, drawn from Immanuel's veins." Just worship as you sing it. Oh, I just love this song.
There is a fountain filled with blood,
Drawn from Immanuel's veins,
And sinners plunge beneath that flood,
Lose all their guilty stain.
Lose all their guilty stain,
Lose all their guilty stain;
And sinners plunge beneath that flood,
Lose all their guilty stain.
text_no:
La oss synge en liten salme nå. Dette er tilbedelse. Vi måtte avslutte litt tidligere, men dette er tilbedelse. La oss nå bli helt stille og synge "There is a fountain filled with blood, drawn from Immanuel's veins." Tilbe mens dere synger. Å, jeg elsker denne sangen.
Det er en kilde fylt med blod,
Trukket fra Immanuels årer,
Og syndere dykker ned i den floden,
Mister alle sine skyldflekker.
Mister alle sine skyldflekker,
Mister alle sine skyldflekker;
Og syndere dykker ned i den floden,
Mister alle sine skyldflekker.

text:
Now, new-born Christian, you that raised your hand just a few moments ago, I want you to promise me something. There's many pastors here in the church, and I want you to get with some of them, some pastor. Promise that to God. Now, you're a Christian now. God's Word is eternally right. "He that heareth My words, believeth on Him that sent Me, has eternal life."
And there's only one type of eternal life. And God alone has eternal life. Is that right? And He gives it to you. But what? "He that heareth My words, and believeth on Him that sent Me, has eternal life (that's without end) and shall never come to the judgment; but hath passed from death unto life."
Now, find you a good church home, quickly. Be baptized, and move into that church, you and your family, and live peacefully with God. And if I never meet you again this side of the river, I'll meet you on the other side. The Lord bless you.
text_no:
Nå, nyfødte kristne, dere som rakte hånden opp for noen øyeblikk siden, vil jeg at dere skal love meg noe. Det er mange pastorer her i menigheten, og jeg vil at dere skal ta kontakt med en av dem. Lov det til Gud. Nå er dere kristne. Guds Ord er evig rett. "Den som hører Mine ord og tror på Ham som har sendt Meg, har evig liv."
Og det finnes bare én type evig liv, og Gud alene har evig liv. Er det ikke riktig? Og Han gir det til dere. Men hva sier Han? "Den som hører Mine ord og tror på Ham som har sendt Meg, har evig liv (det er uten ende) og skal aldri komme til dommen, men har gått over fra døden til livet."
Nå, finn dere et godt menighetshjem raskt. Bli døpt, og bli en del av den menigheten, du og din familie, og lev fredfullt med Gud. Og hvis jeg aldri møter dere igjen på denne siden av elven, vil jeg møte dere på den andre siden. Herren velsigne dere.

text:
Maybe if we have time after the prayer cards are given, and we're praying for the sick tonight, that you can come up around the altar and stand here a little while so the pastors over the city can see who you are.
I let fifty thousand converts go here not long ago. I don't know what happened. In a certain nation that I was in, someone just set up the meetings. We didn't have any cooperation. They just set up the meeting, and thousands poured into that country. And there was no church home to take them to. I don't know what happened.
text_no:
Kanskje, hvis vi har tid etter at bønnekortene er delt ut og vi ber for de syke i kveld, kan dere komme opp rundt alteret og stå her en stund så pastorene i byen kan se hvem dere er.
Jeg lot femti tusen konvertitter gå her for ikke lenge siden. Jeg vet ikke hva som skjedde. I et bestemt land jeg var i, satte noen bare opp møtene uten noen form for samarbeid. Tusenvis strømmet inn i landet, men det var ingen menighetshjem å ta dem til. Jeg vet ikke hva som skjedde.

text:
I thought every church in the country was cooperating. But just a minister … or, a man, just a lawyer, just set up the meeting and said, "Come on." And I just flew in there and started. We had five nights and five thousand each night or more, come to Christ. And I don't know what happened. Whether they went to church home…
I'm learning a little better than that now. Every person that's saved ought to have a church home. They ought to be in church, and there every time that church has a service, or every time you pass it, go in, open the doors and go in and pray or do something. Love the Lord.
text_no:
Jeg trodde hver menighet i landet samarbeidet. Men bare en forkynner ... eller en mann, bare en advokat, satte opp møtet og sa: "Kom igjen." Og jeg fløy bare inn der og startet. Vi hadde fem netter med fem tusen mennesker eller flere hver natt som kom til Kristus. Jeg vet ikke hva som skjedde. Om de gikk til en menighet ...
Jeg har lært litt mer nå. Hver person som blir frelst, bør ha et menighetsfellesskap. De bør være i menigheten, og delta hver gang det er et møte. Hver gang du går forbi menigheten, gå inn, åpne dørene, be eller gjør noe. Elsk Herren.

text:
It was a… Today where I live there was some people had had to leave. There happened to be a very good Norwegian friend of mine who's sitting here. He's my secretary for the Norwegian people, and has been since I was in Norway. And he's done all the translation, not one cent for it or anything.
He was sitting in the room, he and his lovely little wife. And a vision begin to move. And someone knocked at the door, and we went in. And while we were in the room, there were three sick people in there, very sick. The Holy Spirit come down and moved into the room, and told each of them all their troubles, healed them.
text_no:
I dag, der jeg bor, måtte noen dra. Blant dem var en god norsk venn av meg som sitter her. Han er sekretæren min for de norske, og har vært det siden jeg var i Norge. Han har gjort all oversettelsen uten å ta en eneste krone for det.
Han satt i rommet sammen med sin kjære lille kone. En visjon begynte å bevege seg, og noen banket på døren. Vi gikk inn. I rommet var det tre svært syke personer. Den Hellige Ånd kom ned og fylte rommet, fortalte hver av dem alle deres plager, og helbredet dem.

text:
Then just a few minutes later, I run into two ladies. They're sitting right before me now. And it's been said, "Oh, well, he just reads their minds, or there's some way, he… It's a trick he has he does." So these ladies had heard this. Standing right there in the presence of Almighty God, God came right down with a vision and just told all about it, standing right there them, their troubles.
I could ask that lady to raise her hand, if you'd like for to see. Would you like to see the lady? If you would, raise your hand, if you'd like to see the lady. All right, lady. Where was you from? Dawson Creek. Never seen her or knowed of her in my life. I believe this is the other lady sitting here---a Ukrainian lady. Is that right? Isn't that lady right here? Raise up your hand so the people can see.
